ENTER.text: Congrats! you moved to London. Flat is nice. Flatmates are nice too. Are we going to the pub tonight?

Yep!.text: Drank waaaay too much. Getting sleepy.  Good night!

Nah. Going to bed now.text: Straight to bed now, sleep tight

Sleep.text: Night 01. The mattress feels a bit lumpy

Inspect mattress.text: One of the slats seems to have gone out of place

Go back to sleep.text: Back hurts like a bitch in the morning

Next.text: Night 05, You wake up with bites

Inspect bites.text: There's a huge red spot on your temple. It's puffy in the morning but flattens and spreads by the evening. There's a cluster of smaller bites on your shoulder. Very close together.

Google bug bites.text: Search results show bed bugs, fleas, mites. The bite clusters match bed bugs. Fuck.

Go to bed.text: Night 08. You wake up with more bites on your face and back. One is right on your lip

Consult flatmates.text: Flatmate #1 says airing the mattress outside might help with the stress

Lift mattress.text: You lift the mattress to air it out...

Continue.text: There are bugs. Small, brown, flat bugs. Everywhere.

PANIC.text: You shit yourself. Your flatmate shits himself. Your other flatmate is fine. What now?

Stay calm.text: Eh gross. Bugs are bugs. What now?

Contact landlord.text: Your landlord says the bugs probably came with you. He drops off spray cans. 'That should do it.'

Push back.text: You ask for professional pest control. He stops replying to texts.

Fine. DIY.text: You spray everything. Twice. The bites continue.

Contact council.text: Council website: 'Submit request online. Processing takes 2-4 weeks.' Your bites can't wait.

Deal with it yourself..text: Fine. You'll handle this. What's the plan?

DIY.text: You order diatomaceous earth and spray. Week 1: still bites. Week 2: more powder. Week 3: you're checking the mattress TWICE DAILY. Flipping it once a week

Professional.text: £800 for heat treatment. They come Tuesday. You evacuate for the day. That night: new bites. You book another treatment. Your flatmate offers her room while you wait for a new mattress. All your clothes go into plastic bags on the balcony. You iron everything. Then iron them again.

Night 28.text: Night 28. You've been sleeping on the plastic wrap since the mattress arrived. Everything in this room feels contaminated.

Night 35.text: Night 35. You feel crawling on your skin. Static electricity, you tell yourself. But you can't shake the eerie feeling that you're not alone. You can't sleep.

Night 37.text: Night 37. You feel pinpricks. Biting sensations. Every speck of dust could be an egg latched onto your clothes. Even after a hot shower, it's still there. You NEED to see what's underneath.

Cut skin open.text: You dig in with your nails. There's something small, beady, and ugly there—you can present it to your landlord. You're bleeding but clean. The dots are everywhere now.

End.text: A few months have passed. You still flip your mattress once a week. Spray cans and diatomaceous earth at the ready. Your bed is a complete fortress. You've bled money in the process. But your room is yours again. You are safe now.

About Delusory Parasitosis.text: Delusory parasitosis (DP) is a condition where individuals remain convinced of parasitic infestation, despite no evidence. Experiencing pinpricks, biting and crawling sensations.They have extensive knowledge of the parasite they are dealing with in terms of appearance and behaviour. They gather evidence of infestation to prove their sanity, sometimes from their own body. Patients with DP are disproportionately female (Hinkle, 2000). This project explores the psychological aftermath of actual bed bug infestations.
